item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Possible replacement image?

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably, seeing as Wikipedia replaced the image years ago and the original hosting site doesn't specify the owner. As such, it is considered noncompliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anyone else get the impression that, in experiment -3 when D-OCD had his stroke, 1190 transferred the obsession effect to the researcher who wrote that last note?

It's just that while all the observed subjects had personalities tending towards intervention, the good doctor is inclined to being a passive universal curator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The implications of this disturbed me, so of course I upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorites. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting this for the fridge horror relating to the simulation argument and handing the controls over to a confirmed sociopath. Pure gold that.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it introduced a substantial amount of new content without the permission of the author or staff.

Readers, please note that any edits that are not spelling, grammar, or punctuation must be approved by the author or staff before they are implemented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it possible to "kill" an algorithm? What about an algorithm that is programmed to seem lifelike? In this day and age, we do so all the time - sims are an example of this.

Is there a point where an algorithm can be so complex that destroying it is tantamount to murder? Where do we draw the line? And does this mean that in creating this sufficiently complicated algorithm, the designer has created genuine life?

The researchers working on SCP-1190 apparently didn't have enough time to answer these questions before the experiments broke down, but people in the related field of robotics have various (conflicted) opinions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, I think that the Ethics Committee has enough on its plate with more concrete moral issues that affect the world we live in (Procedure 110-Montauk or SCP-089, anyone?) for an issue like this to be on their radar. They are too busy rubber-stamping requests to spend more than a glance at a vague quandary like this. (See here.)

Perhaps if the Foundation were considering a 4th round of experimentation, the Ethics Committee would be involved in the moral implications of exposing a new test subject to a device that has left all prior test subjects dead or disabled, but I think that making moral judgements on the fate of the simulation itself might be too abstract and muddy for an already taxed committee to consider.

On a meta-level, don't forget that there is no canon!

Although integrated into the canon, the Ethics Committee is one of thousands of concepts, many of which could be related to any given SCP. Giving a nod to all of them would make the article unreadable without adding anything to it.

Part of the point of this SCP is the ethical dilemma behind it as well. Bringing in any input from the Ethics Committee would only clarify the intentionally vague ethical implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it doesn't say wether they are alive or not. We should ask the author, but he hasn't been active for two years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being "a simulation", and being "like Sims" are two very different statements. The Sims is a game with objects that have the appearance and some of the behavior of people, but no actual emotions. A sufficiently advanced simulation could fully simulate living brains and emotions, to the point where the simulated beings are living, feeling beings by some definitions. That was what I thought was going on in this SCP. If the Foundation knows that this SCP doesn't actually fully simulate feeling beings, and instead just gives the appearance of doing so, then I think that should be made more clear in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it's just a simulation. It's like killing Sims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing that jumped out at me was that the foundation let billions be tortured just to collect data on how this thing works. I mean, Jesus man.

Actually, now that I think about it, wouldn't that at least warrant a comment from the Ethics Committee and a corresponding response from an O5? Seems kind of inconsistent with generally accepted, and my personal, Foundation canon. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept, but I agree that the logs felt "off." The subjects weren't people, more like shallow characterizations.

Meh overall. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted with a vengeance. The ramifications make my head hurt a little. Anything that can give me a headache I enjoy needs support.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Touches nose and winks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized something that made me upvote this instantly.

Let's make two assumptions. First let's assume that the simulated universe follows the physical laws of our universe with 100% accuracy, and that the initial conditions are identical to those of our own universe. Second, let's assume that in the 1 hour SCP-1190-1-D spent tending to the simulation before suffering his stroke, he was unable to figure out how to actually affect the simulation's progress.

In short, if allowed to continue running, the simulation will generate a perfect representation of a universe with our own Big Bang starting conditions and no intervention from external parties. What if this simulated universe is identical to our own?

The Foundation now has an ongoing experiment that could disprove the existence (or at least intervention) of God.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the feedback. Can you expand on how you felt the diaries and dialog were unnatural? Any specific suggestions how how they could feel more natural?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat, but the diaries and the dialogue in the video transcript felt unnatural. The concept itself is played with very nicely here, but it isn't quite interesting enough for me to upvote in conjunction with the issues I have with the dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My goodness.

I can tell you why the obsessive-compulsive D-17019 had a stroke: he could not comprehend universal entropy. While the universe is tangible, a universe requires a certain level of entropy to even exist as a general rule. For example, it took the sociopathic D-11593 'a long time' to find worlds inhabited by creatures to torture. The very fact that he had to search for more, and not merely create anew, solidifies that some measure of entropy is mandated by the program.

Imagine this: You are compelled to control everything around you to match a certain set of specifications. You are given a machine that is a cognitohazard, compelling you to control it to your ideal specifications. Your ideal specifications include total control of everything to match patterns, but the program doesn't allow for this. You are ensnared by this object regardless. Good luck!

It literally broke his brain with this conflict. Upvoting. Hot damn. And even if this isn't the intended message, it's still a damned good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To what over-reaction are you referring? She certainly over-reacts at the end when the device loses power, but I'm not sure I follow what you mean by an over-reaction in the beginning.

I tried to have her show intrigue in kind of a loopy, artsy-fartsy, crazy-enough-to-kill-you-because-you-vandalized-her-work way, which I concede could easily come off as hammy. Her eccentricity was intentional, because she was pretty nuts before the experiment began.

Another part of the idea is that obsession sets in at different rates and in different ways for different people. The amount of time the artists spends at the simulator escalates quickly, but she still is able to get away from it to write journals. She became obsessed more quickly than the sadist, but the journal was still important to her, so she continued to write in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 2nd D-class to be experimented upon is an artist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're responding to a specific post, use the reply button. Like so.

Also, "the artist"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to disagree with the post over mine… I really didn't like the way the artist is written… at least not the beggining, since the over-reaction can be explained once the obsession sets in, but before that, feels ham-y.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh object, very nice test logs, with very different D-class personalities written in a believable way.
Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I probably didn't make it clear enough; my issue isn't that the image was edited at all, since that doesn't bother me. It's the fact that it stood out to me as a very obvious thing, but it wasn't remarked on at all. When I opened the article, the first thing I saw was the image, and it looked like the computer had kinda… melted into the wall, or something. And then the article itself, although great, didn't mention it at all, as if the image was fine when it's obviously odd in some way.

That said, I appreciate the edit, and have now upvoted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't mean to say that the opinion was out of line. I did surprise me, though, because 1190 doesn't depend on the image. If I removed it entirely, the lack of an image wouldn't be conspicuous.

All kinds of things can cause suspension of disbelief. I just thought that the edited background of the image being a significant detractor was very odd. Either way, I added a note in the caption mentioning the editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're entitled to your opinion, but really? A slightly odd treatment on the background of an image on which the article doesn't depend is enough to make you withhold an upvote for an article you otherwise like a lot?

Suspension of disbelief on SCPs is key. Things which break it can be quite a legitimate source of downvotes. If someone wrote an awesome SCP article and then put a picture of it with a Shutterfly watermark splashed across it, I would downvote it instantly, because the Foundation isn't going to put a hokey stock image in a file. SCP-1960 is currently sitting at negative ratings right now largely because no one can figure out an appropriate image that doesn't call lolcats to mind. I don't necessarily share stickman's opinion on this particular question, but he's certainly not out of line, and he's got a point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The funny thing about that image is that I ganked it from Google image search that way. It was posted on a university website listing past computer systems, and someone there must have gone in with MS Paint or something. I kind of liked the treatment specifically because it looked a little odd in a way that doesn't change the central focus of the image.

You're entitled to your opinion, but really? A slightly odd treatment on the background of an image on which the article doesn't depend is enough to make you withhold an upvote for an article you otherwise like a lot?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one quite a lot, but the image is weird to me. It looks like someone's covered up something on the walls/floor with a photoshop brush/blur tool. Which in and of itself is not anything that'd bother me, but it gives the image a very distinctive look, and it's weird to me that it's not commented on anywhere in the article. The image has clearly been altered for some reason (or the photo was taken in a bizarre as hell room) and it's not mentioned anywhere; it feels like there needs to be a line saying something like "image edited to hide (logo, name, classified information, something)".

Other than that though, I like this one a lot. My issues with the image make me not want to upvote, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, we already have a universal simulator or two. Yes, there isn't a lot of "surprise" in the article, save for the exact way the sociopathic assholes act like sociopathic assholes.

That said I friggin love this thing, it's well written, the science is good enough, and it's an interesting and entertaining read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's nothing all that surprising about the article. It's The Sims taken up to the scale of a universe. Some people are sadistic assholes to the virtual people, some are caring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Just wow. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is good, but the video excerpt and experiment logs where a bit off. They just didn't feel natural at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The artist in medical could be (and probably is) undergoing experiments in medical. I'll see about adding a little more detail to her outcome.

The stroke affected the ventral striatum, which is thought to be involved in motivation and reward1 , the addiction process2 and is also affiliated with OCD3. The suggestion is that both the subject's OCD and compulsion to interact with the machine are correlated with the same area of the brain, which was affected by stroke, and in this case changed the way he subject's compulsions work.

Note that although his OCD is relieved, the subject has lost motivation to do anything.

Footnotes
1. http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17558276
2. http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15019426
3. http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18490925

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject is currently restrained in medical.

I'd think the Foundation would want to find out if the obsession could be cured with amnesiacs, and this would be a perfect test case.

With SCP-1190-1-D's brain damaged, it appears that SCP-1190 no longer is able to exert a compulsive effect on the subject.

Did the SCP cause the stroke, because a subject who's already obsessed isn't compatible with the SCP? Because it seems pretty unlikely that a stroke could cure someone's OCD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want this, just without the negatives. I could enjoy it thoroughly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any ideas what it was that didn't work for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sort of liked that first experiment log, but the rest turns me off a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh…I feel like something's off with this. I don't know what, but something about this just rubs me the wrong way.

However, I'm not going to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's wrong with it? I can't figure out what you mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Interesting and definitely different than SCP-866. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you elaborate? Do you mean the research part of the logs or the transcripts (or both)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't like the "voice" of the experiment logs at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did find SCP-866 after writing but before posting mine, but I felt like the consequences and perspective were different enough to distinguish them. They could be digital cousins though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great logs, great execution, loved the original log with "Killing billions". As for the last log, I am not really sure what it means, but it stands out in contrast and that I like.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion, this article is much more interesting than -866, mainly because it's not an ambiguous mess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coolio. Burn a copy of it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amusing, but feels really similar to SCP-866. Both are computers capable of simulating an entire universe. 866's hook is that it's simulating OUR universe, and the glitches we introduced to it had terrible consequences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoa, man.

…In the best possible way.
